Bonus Q&A: 	The 	Snap 	Suggestion
Part 1

Jon: 	I want to get into questions and answers here, Josh, because we
covered a lot of content here. We’re going to continue the snap
suggestion at the beginning of the next session.
But first, let’s take our 10 minutes to take some Q&A.
So, these are questions specifically related to pattern interrupts, and
step 1, the snap suggestion. Josh?
Josh: 	First question, Gina wants to know what significance is the rainbow on
the fish hat?
Jon: 	It had no significance at all. I literally went to the Web and grabbed the
first fish I could find. It had no significance at all. I could make
something up, but I just wanted a colorful, cartoony fish, and I just
found that and snapped it in there.
But good question. There could have been some significant reference
behind that. But no, none that I’m consciously aware of. Good
question.
By the way, Josh, before we get to more questions, I do want to ask
this, because there was so much dense stuff in that one 30-minute
session we just went through. I hope everyone got a lot out of that.
Don’t worry, it’s recorded. It’s all recorded. So, you’re going to be able
to go back and listen to this and watch it over and over again. It will be
transcribed here, in about a week.

-- 1 of 6 --

2
But I hope that everybody got a lot of stuff out of it, because we
covered some really powerful tips and how you structure certain things.
So, let’s start from there. Did everybody get a lot out of that? And do
you understand, now, how to create a pattern interrupt and why it’s
important? And if you don’t, if you have any questions about that, go
ahead and get them out right now.
Josh: 	Alice is asking, “Jon, do you use testimonials in your video sales
letters?”
Jon: 	We are covering testimonials in the part 4 of the 5-Step VSL process.
Do you need testimonials? Yes and no. I have done VSL’s that have
worked very well without a shred of testimonial, other than myself. It is
the safest way to avoid any issues, if you’re running with a lot of traffic.
It also depends on your brand recognition. If your brand is recognized,
then probably not. If you’re an unknown and you’re not getting a lot of
traffic, definitely, testimonials will definitely help. It’s where you put
them that counts.
Remember, steps 1 and 2 is all about doing 2 things. And please,
write these 2 things down. The entire first 2 steps of the video sales
letter is entirely based on 2 things: 1) snap the user out of their
present state.
Everyone is in a state, when they hit your website. They’re expecting
to see something, and you’re going to give them exactly what they’re
not expecting, but more than they’re expecting, at the same time. You
do that with a pattern interrupt, and then the big promise, which we’ll
cover in the video coming up next. So, that’s what you want to do.
And the other thing is you’re building rapport.
So, if you’re throwing in testimonials right off the bat, bad idea,
because you have no rapport. Trust me when I say rapport is
absolutely key. Once you build rapport, once someone says, “Wow! I
can relate to him or her, that person’s really got their act together when
it comes to blank,” or “wow, that company sounds like they could really
provide the legal services that I’m looking for,” and it could be
corporate or it could be individual, it does not matter, once you get that
rapport, then you can say, “Let me share what clients just like you have
said.” We’ll go over how you word that in the coming sections.
Josh: 	The next one, Zack is asking, “Are there any photos that will actually
turn people off?” He said he’s doing 2 video sales letters, one on body
weight training, another on rest and workouts.

-- 2 of 6 --

3
Jon: 	Oh, yeah. I know Zack. That’s a great question. Definitely. And let
me give you a prime example. I’m not making this up. I was going to
use this as my pattern interrupt for this lesson. Now, I have not used it
live, but remember those 5 things that I told you about, the 5 things I
picked out of my nightmare story. It was a car, a jacket, a store,
prostitutes, and a donut. Well, guess which one of those 5 I was going
to use?
“Hi, this is Jon, and these are crack whores.” Would that be offensive?
It would be offensive to probably 30% of the people listening, I’m sure.
On some level. That’s exactly what happened, though. These were
prostitutes that were, as I said, very sadly, obviously, addicted to
something. I remember that night like it happened yesterday.
So, I avoided that, because I don’t need to. There’s no reason. I don’t
want to offend anyone. I just want to snap them out of their pattern
interrupt.
So, if you use something too racy, if you use something that’s a little
over the top, you run the risk of that.
But remember, a pattern interrupt can be something simple. Let me
use a really good one.
My good friend, Jeff Siegel’s got one of the best-converting video sales
letters ever. And he starts off with a picture of his partner, Isabel,
holding her baby. That’s a very, very sweet and totally innocuous
pattern interrupt.
But when you go to a weight loss site, you might be expecting to see
Isabel like in a bikini or Isabel in a workout uniform, or Isabel flexing, or
something like that. You don’t expect to be seeing Isabel with a baby
and a guy’s voice.
So, they accidentally did this. Jeff admits this. This was not planned.
But that was the most brilliant accidental bit of marketing that I’ve ever
seen. And Jeff is a very smart guy, so it’s not like he couldn’t come up
with that on his own. But that part was an accident.
It was awesome, because he comes on with his voice, “Hello, this is
Jeff, but that’s not a picture of me.” Well, I know that, but who is it?
And it creates a wonderful pattern interrupt.
For example, if I was going to do that, I could say, “Hi, this is Jon. I’m
showing the picture of a beautiful girl holding a cat. I’ll bet you’re
thinking, ‘That’s not you,’ and you’re right. That’s my girlfriend, Sally,
and her cat, George. I don’t have either.

-- 3 of 6 --

4
So, that’s an example. So, I would avoid anything overly controversial,
unless that’s your audience.
There’s a guy that I know that does some very controversial stuff, the
end of the world kind of stuff, the end of the free market, and he wants
to be aggressive.
So, you have to define your audience.
Josh: 	This one’s from Pamela. She says, “For the visual interrupt, does the
success depend on the difference between the image and the benefit
of the product? The more unusual or harder to figure out, the better?”
Jon: 	I think so. I absolutely think so. Let’s go back to Jeff and Isabel’s
example. Isabel holding a baby, that’s as far away from their product
as you can get, really. But you could put 2 and 2 together, if it was her
voice.
If she said something like, “Hi, this is Isabel, and look how fit I am after
giving birth to my baby,” that would be a horrible pattern interrupt,
because that’s what they’re expecting.
But when you’ve got a guy’s voice on top of it, that doesn’t say
anything about, all he says is, “Look how cute! And yes, I’m referring
to the baby,” which is a fantastic, funny pattern interrupt.
So, as far as the images go, this is why we gave you the nightmare
story formula. This is why we gave you the 5 things to pull out of your
nightmare story. And this is an exercise that I want everyone to do.
You can do this during the break. Just take your nightmare story and
pull out 5 objects, 5 elements. In other words, 5 things. If your
nightmare story involved a boat, a cat, a girl, a flyswatter and a bag of
chips, just pull it out. Pull it out. It makes it so much easier.
And then, all you have to do, literally, is find a picture of the most kind
of out-there thing and say, “This bag of chips would be horrible for
weight loss, but this bag of chips is going to teach you how to ski
better, believe it or not.”
During your nightmare story, for whatever reason, you were on a boat
and you were eating a bunch of chips, and you shared it with this guy,
and you got sick eating chips, you had some sort of a tie-in to chips, it
would work perfect. So, I think the answer is yes.
We’ve got time for one more question, Josh.

-- 4 of 6 --

5
Josh: 	A question I’ve seen duplicated 3 times, actually, is do you have any
references for something you think they should study for studying
NLP?
Jon: 	Yeah. There’s tons. You can Google Bandler, and you’ll find Richard
Bandler’s work on NLP. He created NLP. You’ll walk down the chain
of people who know this stuff. Anthony Robbins’ stuff is really good,
when it comes to anchoring and NLP.
The thing is you don’t need to be an NLP practitioner, you don’t need
to be an expert in it. I’m not a practitioner. I just enjoy it. And I enjoy it
not for the things that it can do, because NLP can do things that I, as
an ethical human being, would never do. And I know people that use it
unethically.
I liken it to a firearm. There are people that have weapons, and they
have a license and they go to a place where they shoot their weapon,
and they enjoy it, and it doesn’t hurt anyone. They know how to use it.
A police officer knows how to use it.
In the hands of the wrong person, a weapon can be a disaster. So, the
same thing is true of NLP.
So, yeah, Bandler would be my first choice, because he created it.
And anything he’s written is really, really good.
Anthony Robbins would be the second.
But, again, just to make this very clear, you do not need to know NLP
to write a video sales letter. You just need to know what we’re
teaching you here. Because what we’re teaching you here is based
not just on NLP, but just on general psychology. Do remember, I’m a
psychology minor.
Psychology, in sales, is everything. Everything. So, as long as you
understand the basic psychology that’s going on here, that’s really all
you need to do. We’re opening loops, and things like that. Sure, that’s
an NLP term. But that’s also kind of common sense.
If I say something like, “Okay, in 5 more minutes, I’m going to tell you
where $100 is hidden underneath your couch. I know, because I hid it
there. But it’s not the couch you’re thinking of. I’ll tell you the couch
you’re thinking of in just a few minutes.”
So now, you’re just going, “Okay, I’m going to stick around for a few
minutes because I don’t know which couch he’s talking about.” Right?

-- 5 of 6 --

6
So, that’s kind of common sense. If I wanted you to stick around for a
few minutes, that’s what I would do to make you do that.
But people don’t usually write their copy that way. And, let me just say
this. As another advertisement for why video sales letters – and, by
the way, this is basically the same format as you’d use in a webinar –
there’s only one critical difference, and I will do a bonus video on how
to apply everything we’re teaching to a webinar.
But, you can clearly see that despite the fact that you absolutely need
a text sales page, you do. You need a text sales page, you need a
webinar, you need the video sales letter. They all 3 work.
But, there’s no way you can pull this off on a text-based sales page. If
you said, “I’m going to give you the secret to losing weight that is going
to blow your mind. I’ll give it to you in a few minutes,” they’re just going
to skim the page. In a video sales letter, they can’t skim. Tha’t sthe
power.
Okay, I hope that answers the majority of questions. Any other
questions you have, we’ll catch in the next Q&A session or at the end.
We’ll go a little longer at the end.
So, hopefully, this will get your mind thinking of pattern interrupts. And
while we’re on break, write down those 5 objects. That would just be a
real simple assignment for you. Just write down those 5 objects, so
you can get them in mind, and the circle the one you want to get a
picture of. And it’s as simple as saying, “Hi, my name is Cindy. This is
a blank. And this blank is about to reveal how you can benefit.” It’s
that simple.
